content:
# Numbers Chapter 7 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 154–157; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 116–118 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_007_page_001.jpg` through `NUM_007_page_004.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_007_vaticanus_page_001.jpg` through `NUM_007_vaticanus_page_003.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE DEDICATION OFFERINGS OF THE TRIBAL LEADERS:** Numbers 7 is the longest chapter in the Torah (89 verses) and one of the most deliberately repetitive passages in the Hebrew Bible. The Leningrad Codex devotes four full columns across pages 154–157 to this text, with the formulaic tribal offerings clearly visible in their repeated blocks. The chapter records the offerings brought by the twelve נְשִׂיאִים (nesi'im, "chieftains/leaders") of Israel at the dedication (חֲנֻכַּת, khanukat) of the altar after the tabernacle was erected. Each leader brings an identical offering on successive days, and the text renders each gift in full without abbreviation — a literary choice that honors each tribe's contribution individually rather than reducing them to a summary formula. The Vaticanus LXX (pp. 116–118) follows the MT closely through all twelve iterations, confirming the antiquity of this full-repetition structure. The chapter closes with the grand totals (vv. 84–88) and a remarkable verse (v. 89) in which Moses enters the Tent of Meeting and hears the voice of YHWH speaking from between the two cherubim upon the ark — the culminating purpose of the entire tabernacle project.

---

## The Wagons and Oxen of the Leaders (vv. 1–9)

**1** And-it-was [va-yehi] on-the-day [be-yom] Moses [Mosheh] finished [kallot] setting-up [haqim] [et] the-tabernacle [ha-mishkan], and-he-anointed [va-yimshakh] [et]-it [oto] and-he-sanctified [va-yeqaddesh] [et]-it [oto] and [ve-et] all [kol] its-vessels [kelav], and [ve-et] the-altar [ha-mizbe'akh] and [ve-et] all [kol] its-vessels [kelav], and-he-anointed-them [va-yimshakem] and-he-sanctified-them [va-yeqaddesh otam] —

> **Key terms:** כַּלּוֹת (kallot) = "finishing" — the qal infinitive construct of כלה (kalah, "to complete/finish"), here marking a temporal clause: "on the day of Moses' finishing." הָקִים (haqim) = "setting up/erecting" — the hiphil infinitive of קום (qum). The verse establishes the temporal setting: the events of this chapter follow immediately upon the completion and consecration of the tabernacle (Exod 40:9–11; Lev 8:10–11). The anointing and sanctifying of every vessel establishes the sacred space in which the offerings will be received. **LXX:** ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ᾗ συνετέλεσεν Μωυσῆς ("on the day in which Moses completed") — συνετέλεσεν for כַּלּוֹת, ἔχρισεν ("anointed") for וַיִּמְשַׁח.

**2** And-brought-near [va-yaqrivu] the-leaders of [nesi'ei] Israel [Yisra'el], the-heads of [rashei] the-house of [beit] their-fathers [avotam] — they [hem] [were] the-leaders of [nesi'ei] the-tribes [ha-mattot], they [hem] [were] the-ones-standing [ha-omedim] over [al] the-counted-ones [ha-pequdim] —

> **Key terms:** נְשִׂיאֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (nesi'ei Yisra'el) = "the leaders/chieftains of Israel" — from נָשִׂיא (nasi', "one lifted up/prince"), from the root נשׂא (nasa', "to lift/carry"). These are the same twelve tribal heads listed in Numbers 1:5–16 who supervised the census. הָעֹמְדִים עַל הַפְּקֻדִים (ha-omedim al ha-pequdim) = "the ones standing over the counted ones" — they oversaw the census registration. **LXX:** οἱ ἄρχοντες Ισραηλ ("the rulers of Israel"); οὗτοι οἱ παρεστηκότες ἐπὶ τῆς ἐπισκοπῆς ("these are the ones standing over the visitation/inspection").

**3** And-they-brought [va-yavi'u] [et] their-offering [qorbanam] before [lifnei] YHWH: six [shesh] wagons [eglot] covered [tsav] and-twelve [u-shnei asar] oxen [baqar] — a-wagon [agalah] for [al] two of [shnei] the-leaders [ha-nesi'im] and-an-ox [ve-shor] for-one [le-ekhad]; and-they-brought-them-near [va-yaqrivu otam] before [lifnei] the-tabernacle [ha-mishkan].

> **Key terms:** עֶגְלוֹת צָב (eglot tsav) = "covered wagons" — צָב (tsav) is a rare term, occurring only here and in Isaiah 66:20. The traditional rendering is "covered/enclosed" wagons, suitable for transporting the holy objects. The ratio — one wagon per two leaders, one ox per leader — shows both generosity and coordination. **LXX:** ἓξ ἁμάξας λαμπηνικάς ("six lamp-covered wagons") — λαμπηνικάς is a rare Greek term for a type of covered carriage, corresponding to צָב.

**4** And-spoke [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**5** "Take [qakh] from-with [me-ittam] them [it], and-they-shall-be [ve-hayu] for-doing [la-avod] [et] the-service of [avodat] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed]; and-you-shall-give [ve-natattah] [et]-them [otam] unto [el] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim], each-man [ish] according-to [ke-fi] his-service [avodato]."

> **Key terms:** אִישׁ כְּפִי עֲבֹדָתוֹ (ish ke-fi avodato) = "each man according to his service" — literally "a man according to the mouth of his service," the idiom כְּפִי (ke-fi, "according to the mouth of") meaning "in proportion to." YHWH accepts the offering and directs its use. **LXX:** ἑκάστῳ κατὰ τὴν λειτουργίαν αὐτοῦ ("to each according to his ministry").

**6** And-took [va-yiqqakh] Moses [Mosheh] [et] the-wagons [ha-agalot] and [ve-et] the-oxen [ha-baqar], and-he-gave [va-yitten] [et]-them [otam] unto [el] the-Levites [ha-Leviyyim].

**7** [Et] two of [shnei] the-wagons [ha-agalot] and [ve-et] four of [arba'at] the-oxen [ha-baqar] he-gave [natan] to-the-sons of [li-vnei] Gershon [Gershon], according-to [ke-fi] their-service [avodatam].

**8** And [ve-et] four of [arba'] the-wagons [ha-agalot] and [ve-et] eight of [shemonat] the-oxen [ha-baqar] he-gave [natan] to-the-sons of [li-vnei] Merari [Merari], according-to [ke-fi] their-service [avodatam], by-the-hand of [be-yad] Ithamar [Itamar] son of [ben] Aaron [Aharon] the-priest [ha-kohen].

> **Key terms:** The distribution is proportional: Gershon's clan carried the curtains and coverings (Num 4:24–26), which are lighter; Merari's clan carried the frames, bars, pillars, and bases (Num 4:31–33), which are heavier. Hence Merari receives twice the transport. Ithamar supervised both Gershonite and Merarite service (Num 4:28, 33). **LXX:** κατὰ τὴν λειτουργίαν αὐτῶν διὰ χειρὸς Ιθαμαρ ("according to their ministry, through the hand of Ithamar").

**9** And-to-the-sons of [ve-li-vnei] Kohath [Qehat] he-did-not [lo'] give [natan], for [ki] the-service of [avodat] the-holy-things [ha-qodesh] [was] upon-them [alehem]; upon [al] the-shoulder [ha-katef] they-shall-carry [yissa'u].

> **Key terms:** עַל הַכָּתֵף יִשָּׂאוּ (al ha-katef yissa'u) = "upon the shoulder they shall carry" — the Kohathites carried the most sacred objects (the ark, table, lampstand, altars) on their shoulders with carrying poles (Num 4:4–15). No wagons are given because these items must not touch an unconsecrated surface. The shoulder-bearing preserves the sanctity boundary. **LXX:** ἐπ' ὤμων ἀροῦσιν ("upon shoulders they shall carry").

---

## Day One: Nahshon of Judah (vv. 10–17)

**10** And-brought-near [va-yaqrivu] the-leaders [ha-nesi'im] [et] [the]-dedication of [khanukat] the-altar [ha-mizbe'akh] on-the-day [be-yom] it-was-anointed [himmashe'kh oto]; and-brought-near [va-yaqrivu] the-leaders [ha-nesi'im] [et] their-offering [qorbanam] before [lifnei] the-altar [ha-mizbe'akh].

> **Key terms:** חֲנֻכַּת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (khanukat ha-mizbe'akh) = "the dedication of the altar" — חֲנֻכָּה (khanukkah) from חנך (khanakh, "to dedicate/inaugurate"). This is the same root as the later festival of Hanukkah (1 Macc 4:56–59), which celebrated the rededication of the altar in 164 BCE. הִמָּשַׁח (himmashe'kh) = the niphal infinitive of משׁח (mashakh, "to anoint"). **LXX:** τὸν ἐγκαινισμὸν τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου ("the inauguration of the altar").

**11** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh]: "One [ekhad] leader [nasi'] for-the-day [la-yom], one [ekhad] leader [nasi'] for-the-day [la-yom], they-shall-bring-near [yaqrivu] [et] their-offering [qorbanam] for-the-dedication of [la-khanukat] the-altar [ha-mizbe'akh]."

> **Key terms:** נָשִׂיא אֶחָד לַיּוֹם (nasi' ekhad la-yom) = "one leader per day" — YHWH establishes the order: each tribe presents its offering on a separate day, stretching the dedication over twelve days. The repetition of "one leader per day, one leader per day" emphasizes the deliberate pacing. **LXX:** ἄρχων εἷς καθ' ἡμέραν ("one ruler per day").

**12** And-he-was [va-yehi] the-one-who-brought-near [ha-maqriv] on-the-day [ba-yom] the-first [ha-ri'shon] [et] his-offering [qorbano]: Nahshon [Nakhshon] son of [ben] Amminadab [Amminadav], of-the-tribe of [le-matteh] Judah [Yehudah].

> **Key terms:** נַחְשׁוֹן בֶּן עַמִּינָדָב (Nakhshon ben Amminadav) = "Nahshon son of Amminadab" — the leader of Judah and brother-in-law of Aaron (Exod 6:23). Judah's priority in the offering sequence anticipates the tribe's preeminence in the march order (Num 2:3–9) and later royal destiny. **LXX:** Ναασσων υἱὸς Αμιναδαβ.

**13** And-his-offering [ve-qorbano] [was]: one [akhat] dish [qe'arat] of-silver [kesef], thirty [sheloshim] and-a-hundred [u-me'ah] its-weight [mishqalah] [in shekels]; one [mizraq] bowl [ekhad] of-silver [kesef], seventy [shiv'im] shekel [sheqel] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh]; both-of-them [shneihem] full [mele'im] fine-flour [solet] mixed [belulah] with-oil [ba-shemen] for-a-grain-offering [le-minkhah];

> **Key terms:** קְעָרַת כֶּסֶף (qe'arat kesef) = "a dish/plate of silver" — a large flat serving vessel. מִזְרָק (mizraq) = "bowl/basin" — from זרק (zaraq, "to toss/sprinkle"), a bowl used for collecting and tossing sacrificial blood. בְּשֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ (be-sheqel ha-qodesh) = "by the shekel of the holy place" — the standard sanctuary weight, approximately 11.4 grams. The silver dish weighs 130 shekels (~1,482g), the bowl 70 shekels (~798g). Both are filled with fine flour mixed with oil for the grain offering. **LXX:** τρυβλίον ἀργυροῦν ἕν τριάκοντα καὶ ἑκατὸν ὁλκὴ αὐτοῦ ("one silver dish, one hundred and thirty its weight"); φιάλην μίαν ἀργυρᾶν ("one silver bowl").

**14** One [kaf] spoon [akhat] of-ten [asarah] gold [zahav], full of [mele'ah] incense [qetoret];

> **Key terms:** כַּף (kaf) = "spoon/ladle" — literally "palm/hand," a palm-shaped vessel for holding incense. עֲשָׂרָה זָהָב (asarah zahav) = "ten [shekels] of gold" — the weight of the gold spoon, approximately 114 grams. קְטֹרֶת (qetoret) = "incense" — the sacred blend prescribed in Exodus 30:34–38. **LXX:** θυΐσκην μίαν δέκα χρυσῶν πλήρη θυμιάματος ("one incense-vessel of ten gold [shekels], full of incense").

**15** One [par] young-bull [ben] a-son of [baqar] cattle [ekhad], one [ayil] ram [ekhad], one [keves] lamb [ekhad] a-son of [ben] his-year [shenato], for-a-burnt-offering [le-olah];

**16** One [se'ir] he-goat of [izzim] goats [ekhad], for-a-sin-offering [le-khattat];

**17** And-for-the-sacrifice of [u-le-zevakh] the-peace-offerings [ha-shelamim]: oxen [baqar] two [shnayim], rams [eilim] five [khamishah], he-goats [attudim] five [khamishah], lambs [kevasim] sons of [benei] a-year [shanah] five [khamishah]. This [zeh] [was] the-offering of [qorban] Nahshon [Nakhshon] son of [ben] Amminadab [Amminadav].

> **Key terms:** The offering consists of three categories: (1) Silver vessels with grain offerings — qe'arah and mizraq; (2) Gold incense spoon; (3) Animals — a burnt offering (bull, ram, yearling lamb), a sin offering (he-goat), and peace offerings (2 oxen, 5 rams, 5 he-goats, 5 yearling lambs). The peace-offering animals total seventeen — a generous communal feast. The formula זֶה קָרְבַּן (zeh qorban, "this [was] the offering of") closes each tribal section. **LXX:** εἰς ὁλοκαύτωσιν ("for a whole-burning") for לְעֹלָה; εἰς θυσίαν σωτηρίου ("for a sacrifice of salvation") for שְׁלָמִים.

---

## Day Two: Nethanel of Issachar (vv. 18–23)

**18** On-the-day [ba-yom] the-second [ha-sheni] he-brought-near [hiqriv] Nethanel [Netan'el] son of [ben] Zuar [Tsu'ar], the-leader of [nesi'] Issachar [Yissakhar].

> **Key terms:** נְתַנְאֵל בֶּן צוּעָר (Netan'el ben Tsu'ar) = "Nethanel son of Zuar" — "Nethanel" means "God has given," from נתן (natan, "to give") + אֵל (El, "God"). Issachar was the second tribe in the eastern camp under Judah's standard (Num 2:5). **LXX:** Ναθαναηλ υἱὸς Σωγαρ.

**19** He-brought-near [hiqriv] [et] his-offering [qorbano]: one [akhat] dish [qe'arat] of-silver [kesef], thirty [sheloshim] and-a-hundred [u-me'ah] its-weight [mishqalah] [in shekels]; one [mizraq] bowl [ekhad] of-silver [kesef], seventy [shiv'im] shekel [sheqel] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh]; both-of-them [shneihem] full [mele'im] fine-flour [solet] mixed [belulah] with-oil [ba-shemen] for-a-grain-offering [le-minkhah];

**20** One [kaf] spoon [akhat] of-ten [asarah] gold [zahav], full of [mele'ah] incense [qetoret];

**21** One [par] young-bull [ben] a-son of [baqar] cattle [ekhad], one [ayil] ram [ekhad], one [keves] lamb [ekhad] a-son of [ben] his-year [shenato], for-a-burnt-offering [le-olah];

**22** One [se'ir] he-goat of [izzim] goats [ekhad], for-a-sin-offering [le-khattat];

**23** And-for-the-sacrifice of [u-le-zevakh] the-peace-offerings [ha-shelamim]: oxen [baqar] two [shnayim], rams [eilim] five [khamishah], he-goats [attudim] five [khamishah], lambs [kevasim] sons of [benei] a-year [shanah] five [khamishah]. This [zeh] [was] the-offering of [qorban] Nethanel [Netan'el] son of [ben] Zuar [Tsu'ar].

---

## Day Three: Eliab of Zebulun (vv. 24–29)

**24** On-the-day [ba-yom] the-third [ha-shelishi] the-leader [nasi'] of-the-sons of [li-vnei] Zebulun [Zevulun]: Eliab [Eli'av] son of [ben] Helon [Khelon].

> **Key terms:** אֱלִיאָב בֶּן חֵלֹן (Eli'av ben Khelon) = "Eliab son of Helon" — "Eliab" means "my God is father," from אֵל (El) + אָב (av, "father"). Zebulun was the third tribe in the eastern camp (Num 2:7). **LXX:** Ελιαβ υἱὸς Χαιλων.

**25** His-offering [qorbano] [was]: one [akhat] dish [qe'arat] of-silver [kesef], thirty [sheloshim] and-a-hundred [u-me'ah] its-weight [mishqalah] [in shekels]; one [mizraq] bowl [ekhad] of-silver [kesef], seventy [shiv'im] shekel [sheqel] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh]; both-of-them [shneihem] full [mele'im] fine-flour [solet] mixed [belulah] with-oil [ba-shemen] for-a-grain-offering [le-minkhah];

**26** One [kaf] spoon [akhat] of-ten [asarah] gold [zahav], full of [mele'ah] incense [qetoret];

**27** One [par] young-bull [ben] a-son of [baqar] cattle [ekhad], one [ayil] ram [ekhad], one [keves] lamb [ekhad] a-son of [ben] his-year [shenato], for-a-burnt-offering [le-olah];

**28** One [se'ir] he-goat of [izzim] goats [ekhad], for-a-sin-offering [le-khattat];

**29** And-for-the-sacrifice of [u-le-zevakh] the-peace-offerings [ha-shelamim]: oxen [baqar] two [shnayim], rams [eilim] five [khamishah], he-goats [attudim] five [khamishah], lambs [kevasim] sons of [benei] a-year [shanah] five [khamishah]. This [zeh] [was] the-offering of [qorban] Eliab [Eli'av] son of [ben] Helon [Khelon].

---

## Day Four: Elizur of Reuben (vv. 30–35)

**30** On-the-day [ba-yom] the-fourth [ha-revi'i] the-leader of [nasi'] the-sons of [benei] Reuben [Re'uven]: Elizur [Elitsur] son of [ben] Shedeur [Shedei'ur].

> **Key terms:** אֱלִיצוּר בֶּן שְׁדֵיאוּר (Elitsur ben Shedei'ur) = "Elizur son of Shedeur" — "Elizur" means "my God is a rock," from אֵל (El) + צוּר (tsur, "rock"). "Shedeur" means "Shaddai is light/fire." Reuben led the southern camp (Num 2:10). The shift from the eastern camp tribes (Judah, Issachar, Zebulun, days 1–3) to the southern camp (Reuben, Simeon, Gad, days 4–6) follows the march order of Numbers 2. **LXX:** Ελισουρ υἱὸς Σεδιουρ.

**31** His-offering [qorbano] [was]: one [akhat] dish [qe'arat] of-silver [kesef], thirty [sheloshim] and-a-hundred [u-me'ah] its-weight [mishqalah] [in shekels]; one [mizraq] bowl [ekhad] of-silver [kesef], seventy [shiv'im] shekel [sheqel] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh]; both-of-them [shneihem] full [mele'im] fine-flour [solet] mixed [belulah] with-oil [ba-shemen] for-a-grain-offering [le-minkhah];

**32** One [kaf] spoon [akhat] of-ten [asarah] gold [zahav], full of [mele'ah] incense [qetoret];

**33** One [par] young-bull [ben] a-son of [baqar] cattle [ekhad], one [ayil] ram [ekhad], one [keves] lamb [ekhad] a-son of [ben] his-year [shenato], for-a-burnt-offering [le-olah];

**34** One [se'ir] he-goat of [izzim] goats [ekhad], for-a-sin-offering [le-khattat];

**35** And-for-the-sacrifice of [u-le-zevakh] the-peace-offerings [ha-shelamim]: oxen [baqar] two [shnayim], rams [eilim] five [khamishah], he-goats [attudim] five [khamishah], lambs [kevasim] sons of [benei] a-year [shanah] five [khamishah]. This [zeh] [was] the-offering of [qorban] Elizur [Elitsur] son of [ben] Shedeur [Shedei'ur].

---

## Day Five: Shelumiel of Simeon (vv. 36–41)

**36** On-the-day [ba-yom] the-fifth [ha-khamishi] the-leader of [nasi'] the-sons of [benei] Simeon [Shim'on]: Shelumiel [Shelumi'el] son of [ben] Zurishaddai [Tsuri-Shaddai].

> **Key terms:** שְׁלֻמִיאֵל בֶּן צוּרִישַׁדָּי (Shelumi'el ben Tsuri-Shaddai) = "Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai" — "Shelumiel" means "at peace with God" or "God is my peace," from שָׁלוֹם (shalom) + אֵל (El). "Zurishaddai" means "my rock is Shaddai," from צוּר (tsur, "rock") + שַׁדַּי (Shaddai). Simeon was the second tribe in the southern camp (Num 2:12). **LXX:** Σαλαμιηλ υἱὸς Σουρισαδαι.

**37** His-offering [qorbano] [was]: one [akhat] dish [qe'arat] of-silver [kesef], thirty [sheloshim] and-a-hundred [u-me'ah] its-weight [mishqalah] [in shekels]; one [mizraq] bowl [ekhad] of-silver [kesef], seventy [shiv'im] shekel [sheqel] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh]; both-of-them [shneihem] full [mele'im] fine-flour [solet] mixed [belulah] with-oil [ba-shemen] for-a-grain-offering [le-minkhah];

**38** One [kaf] spoon [akhat] of-ten [asarah] gold [zahav], full of [mele'ah] incense [qetoret];

**39** One [par] young-bull [ben] a-son of [baqar] cattle [ekhad], one [ayil] ram [ekhad], one [keves] lamb [ekhad] a-son of [ben] his-year [shenato], for-a-burnt-offering [le-olah];

**40** One [se'ir] he-goat of [izzim] goats [ekhad], for-a-sin-offering [le-khattat];

**41** And-for-the-sacrifice of [u-le-zevakh] the-peace-offerings [ha-shelamim]: oxen [baqar] two [shnayim], rams [eilim] five [khamishah], he-goats [attudim] five [khamishah], lambs [kevasim] sons of [benei] a-year [shanah] five [khamishah]. This [zeh] [was] the-offering of [qorban] Shelumiel [Shelumi'el] son of [ben] Zurishaddai [Tsuri-Shaddai].

---

## Day Six: Eliasaph of Gad (vv. 42–47)

**42** On-the-day [ba-yom] the-sixth [ha-shishi] the-leader of [nasi'] the-sons of [benei] Gad [Gad]: Eliasaph [Elyasaf] son of [ben] Deuel [De'u'el].

> **Key terms:** אֶלְיָסָף בֶּן דְּעוּאֵל (Elyasaf ben De'u'el) = "Eliasaph son of Deuel" — "Eliasaph" means "God has added," from אֵל (El) + יָסַף (yasaf, "to add"). "Deuel" means "known of God." In Numbers 1:14, the name appears as רְעוּאֵל (Re'u'el, "friend of God") — a ד/ר variant common in Hebrew manuscripts due to the graphical similarity of dalet and resh. The Leningrad Codex reads דְּעוּאֵל here. Gad was the third tribe in the southern camp (Num 2:14). **LXX:** Ελισαφ υἱὸς Ραγουηλ — the LXX reads "Raguel" (= Re'u'el), following the Numbers 1:14 form.

**43** His-offering [qorbano] [was]: one [akhat] dish [qe'arat] of-silver [kesef], thirty [sheloshim] and-a-hundred [u-me'ah] its-weight [mishqalah] [in shekels]; one [mizraq] bowl [ekhad] of-silver [kesef], seventy [shiv'im] shekel [sheqel] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh]; both-of-them [shneihem] full [mele'im] fine-flour [solet] mixed [belulah] with-oil [ba-shemen] for-a-grain-offering [le-minkhah];

**44** One [kaf] spoon [akhat] of-ten [asarah] gold [zahav], full of [mele'ah] incense [qetoret];

**45** One [par] young-bull [ben] a-son of [baqar] cattle [ekhad], one [ayil] ram [ekhad], one [keves] lamb [ekhad] a-son of [ben] his-year [shenato], for-a-burnt-offering [le-olah];

**46** One [se'ir] he-goat of [izzim] goats [ekhad], for-a-sin-offering [le-khattat];

**47** And-for-the-sacrifice of [u-le-zevakh] the-peace-offerings [ha-shelamim]: oxen [baqar] two [shnayim], rams [eilim] five [khamishah], he-goats [attudim] five [khamishah], lambs [kevasim] sons of [benei] a-year [shanah] five [khamishah]. This [zeh] [was] the-offering of [qorban] Eliasaph [Elyasaf] son of [ben] Deuel [De'u'el].

## Day Seven: Elishama of Ephraim (vv. 48–53)

**48** On-the-day [ba-yom] the-seventh [ha-shevi'i] the-leader of [nasi'] the-sons of [benei] Ephraim [Efrayim]: Elishama [Elishama'] son of [ben] Ammihud [Ammihud].

> **Key terms:** אֱלִישָׁמָע בֶּן עַמִּיהוּד (Elishama' ben Ammihud) = "Elishama son of Ammihud" — "Elishama" means "my God has heard," from אֵל (El) + שָׁמַע (shama', "to hear"). "Ammihud" means "my people is glorious," from עַם (am, "people") + הוֹד (hod, "glory/splendor"). Ephraim led the western camp (Num 2:18). Days 7–9 present the western camp tribes (Ephraim, Manasseh, Benjamin). **LXX:** Ελισαμα υἱὸς Εμιουδ.

**49** His-offering [qorbano] [was]: one [akhat] dish [qe'arat] of-silver [kesef], thirty [sheloshim] and-a-hundred [u-me'ah] its-weight [mishqalah] [in shekels]; one [mizraq] bowl [ekhad] of-silver [kesef], seventy [shiv'im] shekel [sheqel] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh]; both-of-them [shneihem] full [mele'im] fine-flour [solet] mixed [belulah] with-oil [ba-shemen] for-a-grain-offering [le-minkhah];

**50** One [kaf] spoon [akhat] of-ten [asarah] gold [zahav], full of [mele'ah] incense [qetoret];

**51** One [par] young-bull [ben] a-son of [baqar] cattle [ekhad], one [ayil] ram [ekhad], one [keves] lamb [ekhad] a-son of [ben] his-year [shenato], for-a-burnt-offering [le-olah];

**56** One [kaf] spoon [akhat] of-ten [asarah] gold [zahav], full of [mele'ah] incense [qetoret];

## Day Ten: Ahiezer of Dan (vv. 66–71)

**66** On-the-day [ba-yom] the-tenth [ha-asiri] the-leader of [nasi'] the-sons of [benei] Dan [Dan]: Ahiezer [Akhi'ezer] son of [ben] Ammishaddai [Ammi-Shaddai].

> **Key terms:** אֲחִיעֶזֶר בֶּן עַמִּישַׁדָּי (Akhi'ezer ben Ammi-Shaddai) = "Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai" — "Ahiezer" means "my brother is help," from אָח (akh, "brother") + עֵזֶר (ezer, "help"). "Ammishaddai" means "my people is Shaddai" or "the Almighty is my kinsman," from עַם (am, "people") + שַׁדַּי (Shaddai). Dan led the northern camp (Num 2:25). Days 10–12 present the northern camp tribes (Dan, Asher, Naphtali). **LXX:** Αχιεζερ υἱὸς Αμισαδαι.

## Day Eleven: Pagiel of Asher (vv. 72–77)

**72** On-the-day [ba-yom] the-one [ashtei] ten [asar] the-leader of [nasi'] the-sons of [benei] Asher [Asher]: Pagiel [Pag'i'el] son of [ben] Ocran [Okhran].

> **Key terms:** פַּגְעִיאֵל בֶּן עָכְרָן (Pag'i'el ben Okhran) = "Pagiel son of Ocran" — "Pagiel" means "encounter of God" or "God has met [me]," from פגע (paga', "to encounter/meet") + אֵל (El). "Ocran" may derive from עכר (akhar, "to trouble/disturb"). Asher was the second tribe in the northern camp (Num 2:27). עַשְׁתֵּי עָשָׂר (ashtei asar) = "eleven" — a unique Hebrew form for this number. **LXX:** Φαγαιηλ υἱὸς Εχραν.

**73** His-offering [qorbano] [was]: one [akhat] dish [qe'arat] of-silver [kesef], thirty [sheloshim] and-a-hundred [u-me'ah] its-weight [mishqalah] [in shekels]; one [mizraq] bowl [ekhad] of-silver [kesef], seventy [shiv'im] shekel [sheqel] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh]; both-of-them [shneihem] full [mele'im] fine-flour [solet] mixed [belulah] with-oil [ba-shemen] for-a-grain-offering [le-minkhah];

**74** One [kaf] spoon [akhat] of-ten [asarah] gold [zahav], full of [mele'ah] incense [qetoret];

**75** One [par] young-bull [ben] a-son of [baqar] cattle [ekhad], one [ayil] ram [ekhad], one [keves] lamb [ekhad] a-son of [ben] his-year [shenato], for-a-burnt-offering [le-olah];

This [zeh] [was] the-offering of [qorban] Pagiel [Pag'i'el] son of [ben] Ocran [Okhran].

## Day Twelve: Ahira of Naphtali (vv. 78–83)

**78** On-the-day [ba-yom] the-two [shnei] ten [asar] the-leader of [nasi'] the-sons of [benei] Naphtali [Naftali]: Ahira [Akhi'ra'] son of [ben] Enan [Einan].

> **Key terms:** אֲחִירַע בֶּן עֵינָן (Akhi'ra' ben Einan) = "Ahira son of Enan" — "Ahira" means "my brother is evil/a friend" — the meaning is debated; it may derive from אָח (akh, "brother") + רַע (ra', which can mean either "evil" or "friend/companion"). "Enan" means "having eyes" or "spring," from עַיִן (ayin, "eye/spring"). Naphtali was the third and final tribe in the northern camp (Num 2:29), closing the twelve-day sequence. **LXX:** Αχιρε υἱὸς Αιναν.

**79** His-offering [qorbano] [was]: one [akhat] dish [qe'arat] of-silver [kesef], thirty [sheloshim] and-a-hundred [u-me'ah] its-weight [mishqalah] [in shekels]; one [mizraq] bowl [ekhad] of-silver [kesef], seventy [shiv'im] shekel [sheqel] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh]; both-of-them [shneihem] full [mele'im] fine-flour [solet] mixed [belulah] with-oil [ba-shemen] for-a-grain-offering [le-minkhah];

**80** One [kaf] spoon [akhat] of-ten [asarah] gold [zahav], full of [mele'ah] incense [qetoret];

This [zeh] [was] the-offering of [qorban] Ahira [Akhi'ra'] son of [ben] Enan [Einan].

## The Grand Totals (vv. 84–88)

**84** This [zo't] [was] the-dedication of [khanukat] the-altar [ha-mizbe'akh] on-the-day [be-yom] it-was-anointed [himmashe'kh oto], from-with [me-et] the-leaders of [nesi'ei] Israel [Yisra'el]: dishes of [qe'arot] silver [kesef] — twelve [shtem esreh]; bowls of [mizreqei] silver [kesef] — twelve [shnem asar]; spoons of [kappot] gold [zahav] — twelve [shtem esreh].

> **Key terms:** The grand totals begin with a summary heading that circles back to the חֲנֻכַּת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (khanukat ha-mizbe'akh, "dedication of the altar") of v. 10. The twelve of each vessel type — dishes, bowls, spoons — mirrors the twelve tribes and the twelve days. **LXX:** οὗτος ὁ ἐγκαινισμὸς τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου ("this [was] the inauguration of the altar").

**85** Thirty [sheloshim] and-a-hundred [u-me'ah] [shekels of] silver [kesef], the-one [ha-akhat] dish [ha-qe'arah]; and-seventy [ve-shiv'im] the-one [ha-ekhad] bowl [ha-mizraq]; all [kol] the-silver of [kesef] the-vessels [ha-kelim] — two-thousand [alpayim] and-four [ve-arba'] hundred [me'ot] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh].

> **Key terms:** The arithmetic: 12 dishes x 130 shekels = 1,560; 12 bowls x 70 shekels = 840; total = 2,400 shekels of silver (approximately 27.4 kg or 60.4 lbs). **LXX:** πᾶν τὸ ἀργύριον τῶν σκευῶν δισχίλιοι καὶ τετρακόσιοι σίκλοι ("all the silver of the vessels: two thousand four hundred shekels").

**86** Spoons of [kappot] gold [zahav] — twelve [shtem esreh], full of [mele'ot] incense [qetoret]; ten [asarah] ten [asarah] the-spoon [ha-kaf] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy-place [ha-qodesh]; all [kol] the-gold of [zahav] the-spoons [ha-kappot] — twenty [esrim] and-a-hundred [u-me'ah] [shekels].

> **Key terms:** עֲשָׂרָה עֲשָׂרָה (asarah asarah) = "ten, ten" — a distributive construction meaning "ten [shekels] each." 12 spoons x 10 shekels = 120 shekels of gold (approximately 1.37 kg or 3.0 lbs). The gold, though lighter in total weight than the silver, was of far greater monetary value. **LXX:** δέκα χρυσοῖ ἡ μία ("ten gold [shekels] the one [spoon]").

**87** All [kol] the-cattle [ha-baqar] for-the-burnt-offering [la-olah]: twelve [shnem asar] young-bulls [par], twelve [shnem asar] rams [eilim], twelve [shnem asar] lambs [kevasim] sons of [benei] a-year [shanah], and-their-grain-offerings [u-minkhatam]; and-he-goats of [u-se'irei] goats [izzim] twelve [shnem asar] for-a-sin-offering [le-khattat].

> **Key terms:** The burnt offering total: 12 bulls + 12 rams + 12 yearling lambs = 36 animals wholly consumed on the altar. The 12 he-goats for the sin offering are listed separately. **LXX:** πᾶσαι αἱ βόες εἰς ὁλοκαύτωσιν ("all the cattle for whole-burning").

**88** And-all [ve-kol] the-cattle of [baqar] the-sacrifice of [zevakh] the-peace-offerings [ha-shelamim]: twenty [esrim] and-four [ve-arba'ah] oxen [par], sixty [shishshim] rams [eilim], sixty [shishshim] he-goats [attudim], sixty [shishshim] lambs [kevasim] sons of [benei] a-year [shanah]. This [zo't] [was] the-dedication of [khanukat] the-altar [ha-mizbe'akh] after [akharei] it-was-anointed [himmashe'kh oto].

> **Key terms:** The peace offering totals: 24 oxen (12 x 2) + 60 rams (12 x 5) + 60 he-goats (12 x 5) + 60 yearling lambs (12 x 5) = 204 animals. Combined with the burnt offerings (36) and sin offerings (12), the grand total is 252 animals sacrificed over twelve days. The closing formula זֹאת חֲנֻכַּת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ אַחֲרֵי הִמָּשַׁח אֹתוֹ ("this was the dedication of the altar after it was anointed") forms an inclusio with v. 84. **LXX:** οὗτος ὁ ἐγκαινισμὸς τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου μετὰ τὸ πληρῶσαι τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ ("this [was] the inauguration of the altar after the filling of his hands") — the LXX's μετὰ τὸ πληρῶσαι τὰς χεῖρας ("after the filling of the hands") is a variant reading, interpreting the anointing as the ordination consecration ritual.

---

## The Voice from the Mercy Seat (v. 89)

**89** And-when-came [u-ve-vo'] Moses [Mosheh] unto [el] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed] to-speak [le-dabber] with-him [itto], and-he-heard [va-yishma'] [et] the-voice [ha-qol] speaking [middabber] unto-him [elav] from-upon [me-al] the-mercy-seat [ha-kapporet] which [asher] [was] upon [al] the-ark of [aron] the-testimony [ha-edut], from-between [mi-bein] the-two [shnei] the-cherubim [ha-keruvim]; and-he-spoke [va-yedabber] unto-him [elav].

> **Key terms:** This extraordinary final verse stands alone as the theological climax of the chapter — and of the entire tabernacle-building narrative stretching from Exodus 25. מִדַּבֵּר (middabber) = "speaking" — the hitpa'el participle of דבר, a reflexive/iterative form suggesting ongoing, self-generating speech. The Masoretic tradition vocalizes this as מִדַּבֵּר rather than מְדַבֵּר (the piel participle), preserving the nuance that the voice "was speaking of itself" — not merely that someone was speaking. הַכַּפֹּרֶת (ha-kapporet) = "the mercy seat/atonement cover" — from כפר (kipper, "to cover/atone"), the gold lid of the ark between the two cherubim (Exod 25:17–22). This verse fulfills the promise of Exodus 25:22: "I will meet with you there, and I will speak with you from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubim which are upon the ark of the testimony." The entire tabernacle project — from the command to build, through the construction, erection, anointing, tribal dedication — reaches its purpose in this single verse: YHWH speaks. **LXX:** καὶ εἰσῆλθεν Μωυσῆς εἰς τὴν σκηνὴν τοῦ μαρτυρίου λαλῆσαι αὐτῷ, καὶ ἤκουσεν τὴν φωνὴν κυρίου λαλοῦντος πρὸς αὐτὸν ... ἀπὸ μέσου τῶν δύο χερουβιν ("and Moses entered into the tent of the testimony to speak with him, and he heard the voice of [the] Lord speaking to him ... from between the two cherubim") — the LXX adds κυρίου ("of [the] Lord"), identifying the speaker explicitly.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **The theology of repetition — why the text does not abbreviate:** Modern readers often ask why Numbers 7 repeats the identical offering formula twelve times rather than stating it once and noting "and each of the remaining eleven leaders brought the same." The answer is both literary and theological. In the ancient Near Eastern dedicatory tradition, each offering is a discrete, unrepeatable act between a specific individual and the deity. To abbreviate Nethanel's offering by saying "same as Nahshon's" would be to say that Nethanel's gift was derivative — a copy — rather than an original act of worship. The text honors each leader and each tribe as standing independently before YHWH. The repetition also creates a liturgical rhythm: the chapter may have been read aloud over twelve days during later commemorations of the tabernacle's dedication, each day's reading highlighting one tribe. The Leningrad Codex scribes copied all twelve iterations without scribal abbreviation, and the Vaticanus LXX likewise preserves every repetition in full — confirming that this textual fullness was understood as intentional from antiquity.

2. **The order of the tribal offerings and the march order of Numbers 2:** The sequence of the twelve leaders follows exactly the camp and march arrangement established in Numbers 2: East camp (Judah, Issachar, Zebulun — days 1–3), South camp (Reuben, Simeon, Gad — days 4–6), West camp (Ephraim, Manasseh, Benjamin — days 7–9), North camp (Dan, Asher, Naphtali — days 10–12). This is not the birth order of the sons of Jacob, nor the order of the census in Numbers 1 (which lists Reuben first as firstborn). The dedication order follows the cultic-military organization of the camp, confirming that the tribal arrangement around the tabernacle was already operative at the time of the altar's dedication. Judah's priority — first to offer — foreshadows the tribe's leadership role throughout Israel's history.

3. **The Deuel/Reuel variant (v. 42):** The father of Eliasaph, the leader of Gad, is called דְּעוּאֵל (De'u'el, "Deuel") in Numbers 1:14 but רְעוּאֵל (Re'u'el, "Reuel") in Numbers 2:14 in the Leningrad Codex. In chapter 7, verse 42, the Leningrad Codex reads דְּעוּאֵל (Deuel). The confusion between dalet (ד) and resh (ר) is one of the most common scribal variants in Hebrew manuscripts, since these two letters are nearly identical in many scripts. The LXX consistently reads Ραγουηλ (Raguel = Reuel) across all occurrences, suggesting that the Greek translator's Hebrew Vorlage had resh throughout. The Samaritan Pentateuch also reads דעואל (Deuel) consistently. This is a minor orthographic variant with no theological significance, but it illustrates the kind of letter-level attention the Masoretes applied to the text.

4. **עֶגְלוֹת צָב (eglot tsav) — the "covered wagons" (v. 3):** The term צָב (tsav) modifying the wagons is a hapax legomenon within the Pentateuch (it recurs only in Isaiah 66:20). Its meaning is debated: (a) "covered/enclosed" — the dominant traditional rendering, suggesting wagons with canopies suitable for protecting the sacred tabernacle components during transport; (b) "litter-wagons" — from a root meaning "to swell" or "to arch," suggesting a domed or arched cover; (c) the LXX renders λαμπηνικάς, a technical Greek term for a specific type of covered carriage used for processional or dignitary transport. The Vulgate has plaustra tecta ("covered wagons"). Whatever the precise design, the text emphasizes that these were special-purpose vehicles for sacred service, not ordinary carts.

5. **The arithmetic of the grand totals (vv. 84–88):** The summary presents: Silver — 12 dishes at 130 shekels each (1,560) + 12 bowls at 70 shekels each (840) = 2,400 shekels (~27.4 kg). Gold — 12 spoons at 10 shekels each = 120 shekels (~1.37 kg). Animals for burnt offering — 12 bulls + 12 rams + 12 yearling lambs = 36. Animals for sin offering — 12 he-goats. Animals for peace offerings — 24 oxen + 60 rams + 60 he-goats + 60 yearling lambs = 204. Grand animal total: 252. The grain offerings and drink offerings accompanying each sacrifice are noted but not totaled. The precision of these figures serves both an accounting function (these are real inventory records of temple property) and a symbolic function: every number is a multiple of twelve, the number of the tribes, emphasizing the unity and completeness of all Israel's participation.

6. **מִדַּבֵּר (middabber) — the self-speaking voice (v. 89):** The final verse contains one of the most theologically significant grammatical forms in the Torah. The participle מִדַּבֵּר is vocalized as a hitpa'el (reflexive) rather than a piel (active), yielding the sense "speaking [of itself]" or "causing itself to be heard" rather than simply "speaking." Rashi notes this: the voice went forth from before the Holy One and Moses came and heard it within the Tent. The Sifrei (Naso 58) explains that the voice descended from heaven to between the cherubim and from there went out to the Tent of Meeting, yet did not pass beyond the Tent — it was powerful enough to shatter cedars of Lebanon yet was contained within the sacred space. The hitpa'el form suggests that the divine speech is self-originating — not a response to Moses' arrival but an ongoing reality that Moses enters into. This verse forms the narrative bridge between the dedication of the tabernacle and the instructions that follow in Numbers 8–10.

7. **חֲנֻכָּה (khanukkah) — dedication (vv. 10, 11, 84, 88):** The word חֲנֻכָּה (khanukkah, "dedication/inauguration") appears four times in this chapter, more than anywhere else in the Torah. The root חנך (khanakh) carries the sense of initiating or training something for its intended purpose (cf. Prov 22:6, חֲנֹךְ לַנַּעַר, "train up a child"). The altar is not merely new; it is being set upon its life-course of receiving Israel's offerings. This same term was later applied to Solomon's temple dedication (2 Chr 7:9) and to the Maccabean rededication of the altar in 164 BCE (1 Macc 4:56–59), which became the festival of Hanukkah. The verbal and conceptual link between Numbers 7 and the later Hanukkah celebration is well attested in rabbinic tradition: the Torah reading for each day of the eight-day Hanukkah festival is drawn from Numbers 7, with the first day reading vv. 1–17 (Nahshon's offering) and the eighth day reading vv. 54–8:4, culminating in the lighting of the menorah.

8. **LXX Vaticanus distinctive readings (pp. 116–118):** The Vaticanus codex preserves the full twelve-iteration structure without abbreviation, confirming the MT's repetitive form as ancient. Notable variants include: (a) Ραγουηλ (Raguel/Reuel) for דְּעוּאֵל (Deuel) in v. 42, as discussed above; (b) λαμπηνικάς for צָב in v. 3; (c) the addition of κυρίου ("of [the] Lord") in v. 89, identifying the speaking voice explicitly as YHWH's; (d) μετὰ τὸ πληρῶσαι τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ ("after the filling of his hands") in v. 88 for אַחֲרֵי הִמָּשַׁח אֹתוֹ ("after it was anointed"), interpreting the anointing through the lens of the hand-filling ordination ritual (cf. Exod 29:9; Lev 8:33). The Vaticanus text is fully legible across all three pages (pa_0116–0118), with the characteristic uncial script in three columns per page, scriptio continua (no word divisions), and nomina sacra abbreviations (κς for κύριος). The chapter boundary marker is visible at the transition from Numbers 6 on pa_0116.
